title: Upstream peers are failing
description: NGINX can be configured to distribute incoming client requests to multiple upstream peers (individual web servers, application servers, or other backend services). This monitor tracks anomalies in the number of failed upstream peers to identify issues.

definition.message:
{{#is_alert}}
## 🚨 What's happening

An anomaly has been detected in the number of upstream peer communication failures for **{{upstream.name}}** (anomaly score: `{{value}}`, threshold: `{{threshold}}`). NGINX is experiencing an unusual number of unsuccessful attempts to connect to or communicate with one or more backend servers.

First triggered at **{{first_triggered_at}}**, active for **{{triggered_duration_sec}}** seconds.
{{/is_alert}}{{#is_recovery}}
## ✅ Recovered

Upstream peer failures for **{{upstream.name}}** have resolved. Current value: `{{value}}`.
{{/is_recovery}}
{{^is_recovery}}
***

## 📈 Impact

Upstream peer failures reduce the pool of available backend servers, increasing load on healthy peers. Users may experience intermittent errors or increased response times as NGINX retries or routes traffic around failed peers.

***

## Runbook

### Initial Troubleshooting Steps

1. **Identify the affected upstream** from the alert (`{{upstream.name}}`).
2. Open [**Metrics Explorer**](/metric/explorer) and inspect `nginx.stream.upstream.peers.fails` broken down by `upstream` to identify which specific peers are failing.
3. Review NGINX error logs for connection-level failures:
   ```bash
   tail -f /var/log/nginx/error.log | grep "upstream"
   ```
4. Test connectivity from the NGINX host to the failing upstream servers:
   ```bash
   curl -v http://<upstream-host>:<port>/health
   ```
5. Correlate with recent configuration changes or upstream service deployments.

### Cause and Resolution

| Cause | Resolution |
| ----- | ---------- |
| Upstream server is down or crashed | Verify the upstream service is running and listening on the expected port; restart if needed. |
| Network connectivity issues | Test connectivity from the NGINX host to the upstream; check firewall rules and network routing. |
| Upstream not responding within timeout | Review `proxy_connect_timeout` and `proxy_read_timeout` in NGINX config; increase if the upstream is legitimately slow. |
| Misconfigured upstream address or port | Verify the upstream block in NGINX configuration has the correct server addresses and ports. |
| Firewall or security group blocking traffic | Check security group rules and host-based firewall (iptables/nftables) on the upstream servers. |

### Related links

* [Documentation](https://docs.datadoghq.com/integrations/nginx/)
* [Metrics Explorer](/metric/explorer)
* [Log Explorer](/logs?query=source%3Anginx)

### Who should be notified?

Assign the appropriate notification handle for this alert (e.g., `@slack-infra`, `@pagerduty-nginx`):
`@your-team-handle`
{{/is_recovery}}
definition.name: [NGINX] Upstream peers fails
